questions.text: What is the sorted version of the array \( A = \{5, 7, 3, 8, 1\} \) after applying the insertion sort algorithm?
questions.explanation:
Insertion sort builds the sorted array one element at a time. For the array \( \{5, 7, 3, 8, 1\} \):
1. Start with 5.
2. Insert 7: \( \{5, 7\} \)
3. Insert 3: \( \{3, 5, 7\} \)
4. Insert 8: \( \{3, 5, 7, 8\} \)
5. Insert 1: \( \{1, 3, 5, 7, 8\} \)

questions.text: The best case running time of the insertion sort algorithm is obtained when the elements of a given Array (A) are:

choices.label: The middle element is the minimum
choices.text: The middle element is the minimum

choices.label: The middle element is the maximum
choices.text: The middle element is the maximum

questions.explanation: In insertion sort, if the array is already sorted, the inner loop comparison fails immediately for each element, leading to a linear time complexity of \( O(n) \).

questions.text: Which of the following sorting algorithms is/are based on the Divide-and-Conquer design paradigm?
questions.explanation: Both Merge sort and Quick sort use the Divide-and-Conquer paradigm. Merge sort divides the array in halves, and Quick sort partitions the array around a pivot.

questions.text: If \( f(n) = \Omega(g(n)) \) and \( g(n) = \Omega(f(n)) \), which of the following is true?
questions.explanation: By definition, if \( f(n) = \Omega(g(n)) \) and \( f(n) = O(g(n)) \) (which is implied by \( g(n) = \Omega(f(n)) \)), then \( f(n) = \Theta(g(n)) \). Because Theta notation is symmetric, both statements are true.

questions.text: Which of the following functions has the worst case complexity (highest growth rate)?
questions.explanation: In terms of asymptotic growth, \( n^n \) grows faster than exponential functions like \( 2^n \), which in turn grow faster than polynomial functions like \( n^3 \).

questions.text: If \( f(n) = \log \log n \) and \( g(n) = \log n \), which of the following statements is true?
questions.explanation: The function \( \log \log n \) grows much slower than \( \log n \). Therefore, \( f(n) \) is asymptotically bounded above by \( g(n) \), making \( f(n) = O(g(n)) \) true.

questions.text: Which one of the following recurrence relations describes a recursive algorithm that halves the input but must examine every item in the input?
questions.explanation: The term \( T(n/2) \) represents halving the input size in the recursive call, and the additive term \( n \) represents the work done at the current level (examining every item).

questions.text: Let \( f(n) = n^2 - n \). Which of the following constant values for \( c \) make the statement \( f(n) \le c \cdot n^2 \) true for all \( n \ge 1 \)?
questions.explanation: For \( n^2 - n \le c \cdot n^2 \) to hold for all \( n \ge 1 \), we can rewrite it as \( 1 - 1/n \le c \). Since the maximum value of \( 1 - 1/n \) approaches 1 (and is 0 at \( n=1 \)), \( c \) must be at least 1. Among the choices, 1 is the only valid value.
